1: YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: The divine instruction frames the crossing not as an emergency improvisation but as a planned trap — YHWH directs the movement of Israel specifically to lure Pharaoh.
2: Pi-hahiroth... between Migdol and the sea... opposite Baal Tzephon: Baal Tzephon = Baal of the North; a Canaanite deity whose shrine stood on the coast. The place names are Egyptian/Semitic hybrid; the exact locations are debated. Strategically, the geography creates a cul-de-sac: the sea ahead, mountains on one side, Egyptian territory behind — the perfect setting for the trap.
3: Pharaoh will say... they are wandering in confusion (nevukhim) in the land: Navakh = to be confused/perplexed; the wilderness has closed in on them (sagar aleihem hamidbar). God deliberately creates a military situation that reads as Israelite incompetence, drawing Pharaoh's overconfidence. The hardening of Pharaoh's heart is accomplished not only internally but by managing circumstances.
4: I will harden (achazzeq) Pharaoh's heart: Piel of chazaq (to make strong/hard); distinct from the earlier Qal forms where Pharaoh's own heart hardened. The divine initiative now takes over what Pharaoh had been doing to himself. I will get glory (e'kkaved) over Pharaoh: Niphal of kavad (to be honored/heavy); kavod (glory) derives from this same root meaning heaviness — what is weighty is glorious. The irony: Pharaoh's 'hardness' (chazaq) and YHWH's glory (kavod) operate from opposing directions; Egypt's heaviness becomes YHWH's glory.
5: The heart of Pharaoh and his servants was turned (nehehpak): Niphal of haphak (to overturn/reverse) — the same root as Sodom's overthrow (Gen 19:25) and the turning of the plague-blood (Exod 7:17). What have we done that we let Israel go from our service (me'avdenu)? Avad = to serve/work; avadim = servants/slaves; avodah = service/labor/worship. The question reveals that Egypt defined itself by Israel's service — the identity of the empire rested on the enslavement of God's son.
6: He made ready his chariot (wayyesor markavto): Asar = to harness/bind; markavah = chariot (from rakav, to ride); the six hundred chosen chariots (shesh-me'ot rekev bachar) — bachar = chosen/elite — represent Pharaoh's rapid-reaction force, his finest military asset.
7: Six hundred chosen chariots and all the chariots of Egypt: The number six hundred echoes the six hundred thousand adult males of Israel (12:37); the military equivalence frames the confrontation as a cosmic imbalance — all of Egypt's chariots against YHWH's unarmed escaping slaves.
8: YHWH hardened (vay'chazeq) the heart of Pharaoh: The divine hardening is now active and explicit; the phrase b'yad ramah (with a high/lifted hand) describes Israel's exit — ramah = to be high/exalted, the same root as the coming victory song's ga'oh ga'ah (he has triumphed gloriously). Israel marches openly, defiantly, under divine protection.
9: The Egyptians pursued (vayirdefu) them: Radaf = to pursue/persecute (the verb of the Psalms' enemies and of Pharaoh's army here and in v.23); horses (susim), chariots (rekev), horsemen (parashim), and army (cheil): the full military vocabulary catalogs the force bearing down on Israel encamped at the sea.
10: The people were very afraid (yire'u me'od) and cried out (yitz'aku) to YHWH: Yara = to fear/be in awe; za'aq = to cry out for help (the same verb as Israel's cry in 2:23 that moved YHWH to act — the cry of the oppressed). The wilderness complaint-cycle begins its first iteration here: fear → cry → complaint → Moses's reassurance → divine action.
11: Were there no graves in Egypt (ha'mibeli ein q'varim b'mitzrayim)? The bitter irony: Egypt is the world's greatest civilization of the dead — its landscape is dominated by tombs and mortuary monuments. The complaint turns Egypt's obsession with death against the exodus. You have brought us out (hotzetanu) — Hiphil of yatza (to bring out); the very verb of the Exodus creed becomes their accusation.
12: Is not this what we said to you in Egypt: 'Leave us alone (chadal mimenu) that we may serve (va'na'avod) the Egyptians'? Chadal = to cease/leave off/desist. The preference for Egyptian slavery over wilderness freedom is the recurring spiritual failure of the Exodus generation. Better to be well-fed slaves than hungry freedmen — the logic that Jesus addresses in John 8:33-36.
13: Do not fear (al-tira'u), stand firm (hitzyabu): Hithpael of yatzav (to station oneself, take a stand); the reflexive form emphasizes deliberate action — don't just stop shaking, plant yourself. See the salvation (yeshuah) of YHWH: Yeshuah from yasha (to save/deliver) — the same root as Joshua and Jesus. Moses's command is to watch YHWH do the fighting; the salvation belongs to YHWH, not to military resistance. You shall never see them again: The double negative (lo tosifun lir'otam) is absolute — the enemy that pursues you will be annihilated, not merely repelled.
14: YHWH will fight (yilachem) for you, and you only need to be silent (tacharishun): Lacham = to fight/do battle; charash = to be silent, still, to plow (the metaphor of a plowed field — quiet, open, ready to receive). This verse is the Exodus's greatest faith-statement: the battle posture called for is non-resistance. The same theology of divine warfare appears in 2 Chr 20:15-17 (Jehoshaphat) and Isa 30:15 ('in quietness and trust is your strength').
15: Why do you cry to me (mah titz'aq elai)? YHWH's surprising rebuke of Moses's prayer; the time for prayer is past — the time for action has come. The intersection of human prayer and divine command: YHWH has already determined to act; Moses's role is to execute, not to petition. Tell the people to go forward (yisa'u): Qal imperfect of nasa (to set out/journey); the same verb used for the camp's movement throughout the wilderness itinerary.
16: Lift up your staff (harem et-matte'cha) and stretch out your hand over the sea: The staff (mateh) has been the instrument of every plague sign; now it performs the ultimate sign. The gesture of outstretched hand (natah yad) over water is the signature action of Moses throughout chapters 7-14. The staff connects the Exodus miracles thematically — one instrument, many demonstrations.
17: I will harden (m'chazeq) the hearts of the Egyptians: Piel participle — the continuous divine action maintains Pharaoh's pursuit into the trap. And I will get glory (ve'ekkavedah) over Pharaoh and all his host: The kavod/kavad theme reaches its climax — all the weight of the Egyptian military will become the glory of YHWH when the sea swallows it.
18: The Egyptians shall know (yede'u) that I am YHWH (ani YHWH): The da'at-YHWH formula (to know YHWH experientially) is the purpose clause of the Exodus as a whole (3:19-20; 6:7; 7:5,17; 8:10; etc.). Egypt's knowledge is not salvific but judicial — they will know YHWH as the one who judges empire. When I have gotten glory (behikaveddi) over Pharaoh: The Niphal infinitive construct — when I am glorified, honored, made heavy with the weight of his destruction.
19: The angel of God (malach ha-Elohim) who was going before the host of Israel moved and went behind them: The mal'ach ha-Elohim (angel/messenger of God, compare to mal'ach YHWH earlier) = the divine representative, perhaps identical to the pillar. The tactical shift from vanguard to rearguard is a military repositioning — the divine escort becomes the divine shield, covering the retreat. The same cloud that guides Israel becomes the darkness that blinds Egypt.
20: Darkness and cloud to one, light and fire to the other: The same phenomenon serves opposite purposes — judgment and protection from the same divine presence. The pillar is not morally neutral; it discriminates according to the covenantal status of the parties. The wilderness darkness echoes the plague of darkness (10:21-23) where Egypt was in darkness while Israel had light.
21: A strong east wind (ruach qadim azzah) all night: Ruach = wind/breath/spirit; qadim = east (the desert wind); azzah = strong. The wind is the instrument but YHWH is the agent — the text carefully distinguishes the natural means (wind) from the supernatural cause (YHWH drove the sea back). Dry land (choravan): From charev (dry/desolate); the sea floor becomes desert. The creation echo: in Gen 1:2 the ruach Elohim (Spirit/wind of God) moves over the waters before dry land (yabbashah) appears; here the ruach YHWH parts the waters and dry land appears again — Exodus as new creation.
22: Israel walked on dry ground (yabbashah) through the sea: Yabbashah = dry land (Gen 1:9-10 — the term for dry land at creation). The creation-exodus typology is explicit: Israel passes through the parted waters to new life just as creation emerged when the waters were separated. Waters being a wall (chomah) to their right and left: Chomah = defensive city wall; the waters that threatened now protect, flanking Israel as a fortification.
23: The Egyptians pursued and went in after them into the midst of the sea: The Egyptians enter the creation-space Israel has been given — they trespass into YHWH's protected zone. Their confidence (the hardened heart) drives them into the trap.
24: In the morning watch (b'ashmuret haboqer) YHWH looked down (vayashkef) through the pillar of fire and cloud: Ashmuret = watch of the night; haboqer = morning (the period from roughly 2am to sunrise); shakaf = to look down from above. He threw into panic (vayaham): Hamam = to throw into confusion/panic (the same verb used for YHWH routing armies in Deut 7:23 and Josh 10:10); the divine gaze is itself a weapon.
25: He clogged/bound their chariot wheels (vayaser et-ofan markevotav): Asar = to bind/remove; ofan = wheel; the precise mechanism (wheels coming off or sinking) is less important than the theological point: Egypt's greatest technological advantage — the chariot — is disabled by YHWH. They drove heavily (bichvedut): From kaved (heavy) — the root of kavod (glory); Pharaoh's heavy heart now makes his army drive heavily. The Egyptians recognize their situation: 'YHWH fights for them against Egypt' — the enemy confesses the theology of the Exodus.
26: Stretch out your hand over the sea, that the water may come back: The gesture that parted the sea now reverses the division — Moses's hand mediates both the opening and the closing. The created order has been temporarily suspended; now it is restored, but not before its purpose is accomplished.
27: The sea returned (vayashov) to its normal flow when morning appeared: Shuv = to return; the sea 'returns' as YHWH intended — the temporary suspension of natural order served its redemptive purpose. YHWH shook out (vayena'er) the Egyptians into the sea: Na'ar = to shake out (as one shakes a garment to empty it); the image is of YHWH as one who shakes the enemies of Israel out of existence the way dust is shaken from a cloak (cf. Job 38:13 — the earth shaken so the wicked are shaken out of it).
28: Not one of them remained (lo-nishar bahem ad-echad): The total annihilation formula — every chariot, every horseman, every soldier of Pharaoh's elite force. The army that enslaved Israel for four hundred years is destroyed in a single night. The asymmetry between the means (an east wind, two outstretched hands) and the result (the total destruction of the Egyptian military) defines divine warfare.
29: The people of Israel walked on dry ground through the sea: The repetition from v.22 forms a frame around the Egyptian destruction, emphasizing the contrast: the same waters that were walls of protection for Israel were walls of destruction for Egypt.
30: Thus YHWH saved (vayyosha') Israel that day: The Hiphil of yasha — the salvation verb; yeshuah (salvation) in the abstract, yasha in the concrete act. Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the seashore (al-sfat hayam): The body count on the beach is the visible evidence of the invisible battle; seeing the dead is the first step toward the climactic faith-statement of v.31.
31: Israel saw the great power (yad gedolah — great hand) that YHWH used against Egypt: The yad (hand) motif — the outstretched arm of YHWH — reaches its climax in this verse. They feared YHWH (vayire'u et-YHWH) and believed (vaya'aminu) in YHWH and in his servant Moses: The sequence — seeing → fearing → believing — is the Exodus paradigm for faith formation. Aman = to believe/trust, from the root meaning to be firm/reliable (the root of amen); faith is the response to evidence of YHWH's reliability. The Exodus functions as the founding demonstration of YHWH's trustworthiness, referenced throughout the Psalms and prophets as the ground of all future confidence.
